coredata.dc:title: Development of an accurate hand-held sensing platform for nitrite detection based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Liucheng
Zhang, Yan
Du, Liyue
Xu, Shengrui
Gao, Jie
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: As is well known, excessive nitrite can seriously pollute the environment and can harm human health. Although existing methods can be used to determine nitrite content, they still have some drawbacks, such as relatively complicated operation and expensive equipment. Herein, a hand-held sensing platform (HSP) for NO2 − determination was developed. First, ammonia-rich nitrogen-doped carbon dots with orange-yellow emission were designed and synthesised, which were suitable as fluorescent probes because of their good optical properties and stability. Then, the HSP based on fluorescence using photoelectric conversion technology was designed and manufactured using three-dimensional printing technology. Under optimum conditions, the voltage (V/V0) of the proposed HSP showed good linearity for NO2 − detection in the range of 10–500 μM, with a detection limit of 1.95 μM. This portable sensor showed good stability, accuracy and reliability in detecting actual water and meat samples, which may ensure food safety in practical applications. Moreover, the HSP is compact, portable and easily assembled and is suitable for on-site real-time detection, which shows great application potential and prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hand-held sensing platform
Nitrite detection
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Field assay
Fluorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of NO2 − using N-CDs based on fluorescence spectrophotometer
Detection of NO2 − in actual samples using the HSP
Investigation of the quenching mechanism of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of HSP appearance (a). Schematic of HSP internal structure (b).

TEM diagram of N-CDs; the inset was the particle size distribution of N-CDs (a). Two-dimensional AFM image of N-CDs (b). Height profile along the line on the two-dimensional AFM image (c). Three-dimensional AFM image of N-CDs (d).

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs; the inset was photos of N-CDs in daylight (left) and UV light (right) (a). UV–vis spectra of N-CDs and 2,3-diamino pyridine (b). FTIR spectra of N-CDs (c). 1H NMR spectrum of N-CDs and N-CDs + D2O in DMSO‑d 6 (d).
XPS spectrum of N-CDs (a) and the corresponding high-resolution XPS C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) spectra of the N-CDs.

Emission spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs with NO2 −. Inset: optical images of N-CDs (left) and N-CDs + NO2 − (right) under UV light (a). Linearity of F/F0 and NO2 − concentration (fluorescence spectrophotometer method) (b). Schematic of the HSP principle (c). Linearity of V/V0 and NO2 − concentration via HSP (d).

UV–vis spectra of N-CDs (red line), N-CDs + NaNO2 (blue line) and NaNO2 (black line) (a). The first derivative diagram of the UV–vis spectra of N-CDs + NaNO2 (b). Fluorescence decay traces of N-CDs and N-CDs + NO2 − with different concentrations (c). 1H NMR spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + NO2 − (d). FTIR spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + NO2 − (e). Zeta potential of the N-CDs before and after adding NO2 − in 1.0 mM HCl (f).
Schematic of the preparation of N-CDs and its application in the HSP and fluorescence sensor for NO2 − detection.
Results obtained using the HSP and the commercial detector for different samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was mg/L.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was mg/kg.

head.ce:title: Development of an accurate hand-held sensing platform for nitrite detection based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As is well known, excessive nitrite can seriously pollute the environment and can harm human health. Although existing methods can be used to determine nitrite content, they still have some drawbacks, such as relatively complicated operation and expensive equipment. Herein, a hand-held sensing platform (HSP) for NO2 − determination was developed. First, ammonia-rich nitrogen-doped carbon dots with orange-yellow emission were designed and synthesised, which were suitable as fluorescent probes because of their good optical properties and stability. Then, the HSP based on fluorescence using photoelectric conversion technology was designed and manufactured using three-dimensional printing technology. Under optimum conditions, the voltage (V/V0) of the proposed HSP showed good linearity for NO2 − detection in the range of 10–500 μM, with a detection limit of 1.95 μM. This portable sensor showed good stability, accuracy and reliability in detecting actual water and meat samples, which may ensure food safety in practical applications. Moreover, the HSP is compact, portable and easily assembled and is suitable for on-site real-time detection, which shows great application potential and prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is an inorganic nitrogen compound abundant in nature [1]. It is mainly derived from the oxidation of ammonia and the reduction of nitrate [2]. In daily life, nitrite can be used both as food additive and preservative to enhance the flavour and colour of food [3], as well as medicine to treat diseases related to haemoglobin, such as heart diseases and cerebral aneurysms [4]. However, the absorption of excessive nitrite into the body poses a serious hazard to human health [5]. This is because nitrite can oxidise the haemoglobin in the blood, rendering it incapable of transporting oxygen, thereby triggering ischaemic anaemia, respiratory distress and even death [6]. In addition, nitrite can react with secondary amines to produce highly carcinogenic nitrosamines under acidic conditions whether in vivo or in vitro. Nitrosamines have strong teratogenic and mutagenic effects, which can enter the foetus through the placental barrier, causing foetal malformations, premature birth, growth retardation, developmental defects and so on [5]. To protect food safety and human health, the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency set the maximum contaminant levels of nitrite in drinking water at 3.0 and 1.0 mg/L, respectively [7]. Therefore, it is of great significance to exploit simple, sensitive and selective NO2 − detection methods [8].

To date, many conventional laboratory methods have been used to determine the concentration of NO2 −, including electrochemistry [9], chromatographic analysis [10] and Raman spectroscopy [11]. Although these methods present excellent sensitivity and accuracy, they are not suitable for on-site rapid assays because they usually require cumbersome equipment and operation by well-trained professionals. In particular, the cost of equipment will greatly limit the application of these traditional methods in less-developed regions and countries [12]. Recently, the development of portable devices has aroused great interests in analysis fields. Gu et al. [13] used a miniaturised analytical device based magneto-electrochemical detection module to detect ATP and Hg2+ simultaneously. Another portable equipment based fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for the quantitative analysis of iron ions was reported by Liu et al. [14]. Roberto et al. [2] reported ambient light sensors to detect NO2 − in river water. Because of its excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, wide linear range and real-time monitoring ability, fluorescence sensing has gained wide acceptance and has broad application prospects in portable sensing. However, existing fluorescence sensors mainly rely on a fluorescence spectrometer. There is a high demand to develop portable sensor based fluorescence for conveniently detecting NO2 − [15].

In the development of portable sensors, the sensing unit is the most essential part. Organic dyes [2,16], metal nanoclusters [17,18] and fluorescent nanoparticles [19] have been used as fluorescence probes. However, their potential toxicity has raised concerns. By contrast, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, stability, simple preparation and other advantages, making them widely used as sensing units in biological analysis and biological imaging. In particular, the surface of CDs can be modified using various functional groups (amine, carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc.) to mediate chemical reactions for detection purposes [20–22]. For example, the amine groups on the surface of CDs were commonly used as reaction sites and recognition receptors in probe design for detecting NO2 − through the diazotisation reaction between nitrite and the amine group of CDs under acidic conditions, which changed the fluorescence intensity of CDs [19,22]. Ning et al. [23], Rong et al. [24] and Zhang et al. [25] synthesised doped CDs and established effective methods for detecting NO2 −, but these strategies mainly relied on a large and not portable fluorescence spectrophotometer, which needed to be sampled and sent to the laboratory before the detection could be completed [17,22]. Improper storage and transportation of samples after collection might lead to losses due to their chemical instability. Therefore, it was of great significance to develop an inexpensive hand-held detection device to replace the expensive and non-portable fluorometer to meet the needs of people for in situ detection, especially in a field far from the laboratory and in economically underdeveloped areas.

In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) rich in amino groups were synthesised from 2,3-diamino pyridine using the hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). In the presence of nitrite, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased because of the diazotisation of the surface functional groups of N-CDs with NO2 −. Thus, a hand-held sensing platform (HSP) was designed and manufactured using three-dimensional (3D) printing technology. Compared with the previous detection methods in Table S1, the limit of detection (LOD) of our method was lower than that of other methods, such as capillary electrophoresis based on successive multiple ionic-polymer layer coating with a modification of polybrene/dextran sulfate/polybrene [26], electrochemical method based on Au–Dy2(WO4)3 [18], electrochemical method based on CuAg nanoparticles [27], fluorometry based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots [25] and fluorometry based on UiO-66-NH2 [28]. Although some detection methods had low LOD, they did not have portable sensors and could not realise in situ detection. Our portable sensor could convert the change in fluorescence intensity into electrical signal using photoelectric conversion technology to realise a quantitative analysis of NO2 −. When NO2 − was present, the voltage of the HSP decreased, which was consistent with that of fluorescence. Accordingly, an accurate HSP was successfully prepared for highly sensitive and selective detection of NO2 −.
All reagents and apparatus used in this study were detailed in the Supplementary Information (SI).

N-CDs were synthesised using a simple eco-friendly hydrothermal process. Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 showed that the optimal preparation process of N-CDs was a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 8 h. Briefly, 306.0 mg of 2,3-diamino pyridine and 30 mL of ultrapure water were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave and sonicated for 30 min to obtain a well-dispersed suspension. The as-prepared suspension was reacted in a special autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling at room temperature and centrifuging at 12000 r/min for 20 min in a centrifuge to separate non-fluorescent deposits, a pale brown solution was obtained. Subsequently, the supernatant was separated using a 0.22 μm filter to separate insoluble solid particles, the filtrate was dialysed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off 500 Da) for 24 h and the dialysis medium was changed every 6 h. Black N-CDs were obtained after drying.

As shown in Fig. S3, the optimum reaction time between N-CDs and NO2 − was 20 min. Nitrite sample solutions with different concentrations were prepared using 1.0 mM nitrite solution and 0.65 mg/mL N-CDs solution to measure the standard curve. They were vortex mixed for 20 min. Then, the spectrum obtained using a fluorometer and voltage measured using the HSP were recorded. The pH stability and interference experiments of N-CDs were performed according to the methods in SI.

The internal structural layout of the HSP was designed using the 3D modelling software Rhino 6.0, and the HSP was prepared using easily available plastic and 3D printing technology. In addition, to improve the portability of the equipment, the internal structure was gradually optimised and the size of the equipment was reduced to better adapt to on-site detection far from the laboratory. The optimised HSP with a weight of only 411.3 g was a cuboid form, and its length, width and height were 15.7, 9.0 and 6.0 cm, respectively, which was more suitable for on-site detection than the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. 1 a, Fig. S4 and supporting movie 1). As shown in Fig. 1b and supporting movie 2, the HSP was composed of seven cavities, namely, excitation device, photoelectric conversion device, voltage detection device, display, power supply, heat dissipation device and sample support. The first one could generate excitation light, and its size was perfectly the same as that of an LED. The second was a fluorescence detection cavity that could convert the fluorescence signal into voltage signal, including a photoelectric converter and a driving circuit. The two cavities were made of opaque plastic material and connected in series on the sample holder, wherein the detection cavity was perpendicular to the direction of the excitation cavity. The third cavity was the battery compartment, which provided power for the HSP. The fourth cavity was a voltage detection device, which was used to measure the intensity of the voltage signal. The fifth cavity was a voltage display for displaying voltage signals. The sixth cavity was a heat sink, which was mainly used to cool the HSP. The last cavity was a sample holder made of a transparent quartz tube with a diameter of 12 mm, which was used to fix the sample cell. A transparent quartz test tube with a diameter of 10 mm was used as the sample cell. The seven parts were closely connected, independent and working together to realise the in situ detection function of the HSP.

The fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by NO2 −. Their relation was described using the Stern-Volmer equation at low concentrations (Equation S1) [29]. Thus, the concentration of nitrite was indirectly measured using the fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs. When NO2 − was added, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was weakened, resulting in changes in the electrical properties of the photoelectric converter that detected fluorescence intensity. Through the voltage displayed via HSP, the concentration of NO2 − in the sample was calculated.

The water samples of tap water, Weihe River water, Dingguo Lake water, aquarium water and aquaculture water were obtained following ISO 5667–6:2014 and ISO 5667–4:2016 for analysis, respectively [2]. The meat samples were processed according to the ISO 2918:1975 procedure [30]. The HSP was used to determine nitrite in samples (see supporting movie 3), and the results were compared with those of a commercial nitrite analyser (Shenzhen Tongao Tech Co., Ltd.). The accuracy of the HSP measurement results was verified using one-way ANOVA results obtained using Excel software.

Fig. 2 presented the typical surface morphology of the synthesised N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the N-CDs had quasi-spherical shapes with a normal particle size distribution. The particle size was mainly in the range of 2.3–3.7 nm, with an average particle size of 2.94 nm. The AFM images of the N-CDs shown in Fig. 2b–d indicated that the N-CDs had a topographic height in the range of 0.85–1.52 nm, indicating that most of the N-CDs consisted of 2–4 graphene layers [20,31–33]. The SEM/EDS elemental mapping images in Fig. S5 confirmed that the N-CDs were uniformly distributed with C, N and O atoms. As shown in Fig. S6, the hydrochloric acid solution of the N-CDs remained stable within 5 days.

The excellent optical properties of the N-CDs were the basis for the determination of NO2 −. The N-CDs with orange-yellow fluorescence had two excitation wavelengths, which were located at 530 and 565 nm, respectively, both emission peaks were located at 590 nm and the FWHM was only approximately 63 nm (Fig. 3 a). The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931 chromaticity (CIE) colour coordinates of the N-CDs were (0.59, 0.41) in the orange-yellow light region (Fig. S7). The experiment used 530 nm as the excitation wavelength to avoid its influence on emission. The QY of the N-CDs (aqueous solution) was as high as 26.46 % (Fig. S8), and the fluorescence lifetime was 3.97 ns. As shown in Figs. S9 and S10, the N-CDs had low toxicity and could be used for cell imaging.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum was used to evaluate the optical properties of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b and the chemical structure of 2,3-diamino pyridine, the peak at 237 nm was the n→σ* transition absorption peak generated by the lone pair on the amino group and the peak at 301 nm was the n→π* absorption peak generated by the lone pair in the non-bond orbit of the pyridine ring [34]. Compared with the raw material (2,3-diamino pyridine), the absorption peaks of the n→σ* transition (λ = 241 nm) and n→π* transition (λ = 308 nm) of the N-CDs were both red-shifted, which indicated that the conjugated system increased after hydrothermal treatment. Moreover, a new absorption peak was found at λ = 413 nm. The UV–vis results proved the successful synthesis of N-CDs.

In the Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the N-CDs (Fig. 3c), the peaks located at 3356 and 3329 cm−1 belonged to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of N–H, the peaks located at 1598 and 752 cm−1 were from the in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibration absorptions of N–H and the peak located at 1250 cm−1 was contributed by C–N [35,36], respectively, which indicated that amino groups existed on the surface of the N-CDs. The peak at 3180 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, the peaks at 1456 and 917 cm−1 were attributed to the in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibration absorption of –OH and the peak located at 1761 cm−1 was contributed by –CO [37], which indicated that carboxyl and hydroxyl groups existed on the surface of the N-CDs.

NMR is an effective method used to study active hydrogen. The 1H NMR of N-CDs before (Fig. 3d, black line) and after D2O exchange (Fig. 3d, blue line) showed that the peak with a chemical shift of 6.18 was the active hydrogen in the amino group of N-CDs and the hydrogen atoms at chemical shifts of 7.26, 6.82 and 6.51 were aromatic, which showed that there were Ar–H and Ar–NH2 on the surface of N-CDs [35].

The elemental composition of N-CDs was further explored using XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 4 ). The XPS full spectrum analysis showed that the main elements of N-CDs were carbon (56.34 %), nitrogen (28.76 %) and oxygen (14.9 %), which corresponded to three peaks at 284.7, 399.0 and 532.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 4a). There were five peaks at 284.5, 285.7, 286.8, 287.6 and 288.9 eV in the C1s high-resolution spectrum, which corresponded to C–C/CC, C–N, CN, CO and C–O, respectively (Fig. 4b) [38,39]. The high-resolution analysis of N1s showed that the peaks at 399.1, 400.6 and 406.4 eV corresponded to C–N, CN and N–H (Fig. 4c) [40]. In the high-resolution analysis of O1s, two peaks at 531.9 and 540.4 eV corresponded to CO and C–O–C/C–O–H, respectively (Fig. 4d) [41]. The results of XPS were consistent with those of FTIR, which indicated the successful synthesis of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NO2 − using N-CDs based on fluorescence spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: According to a previous study [42], N-CDs with (–NH2, –COOH) groups on the surface were easily influenced by pH, which usually affected the optical properties of CDs. In this study, the fluorescence properties of the as-prepared N-CDs were not affected by pH. As shown in Fig. S11, in the pH range of 3–9, the fluorescence property of N-CDs remained unchanged, and even at pH 10, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs were still above 84.1 %.

Natural water often contains amino acids and inorganic ions from the excreta of aquatic animals, residual bait, dead animals and plants. Meanwhile, processed meat also contains a lot of amino acids and inorganic ions. Therefore, it is necessary to study the interference of common amino acids and inorganic ions. The fluorescence of N-CDs remained unchanged after adding different amino acids (Fig. S12), and the lowest fluorescence intensity could be kept above 96.1 %. In the range of 0–800 mM, the fluorescence properties of N-CDs were not affected by ion intensity (Fig. S13). As shown in Fig. S14, NO2 − could significantly quench the fluorescence of N-CDs and other common ions did not affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, which also proved that N-CDs had good selectivity for NO2 −. The results of pH stability, amino acid and inorganic ion interference showed that N-CDs were an excellent NO2 − detection probe and had good application prospects.

As shown in Fig. 5a and Fig. S15, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched by NO2 − and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased linearly with increasing NO2 − concentration from 0.2 to 0.5 μM, which could be described using the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 5 b) [43]. The equation was F/F0 = −0.00243X + 1.33226 (R2 = 0.9909), and the LOD was 1.6 nM (see SI for details). Compared with the reported methods in Table S1, the established fluorescence method had better analytical ability for NO2 − and lower LOD.

To determine the concentration of NO2 − using the HSP, an indirect path was designed, which allowed the excitation light emitted by the source to excite the sample to emit fluorescence after the unnecessary stray light was removed using the optical filter. The fluorescence signal emitted by the sample was converted into an easy-to-detect voltage signal using the photoelectric converter in the vertical direction, and the voltage signal was displayed using the display for easy observation and recording. In short, the fluorescence signal of the sample was converted into an electrical signal through an excitation light-filter-sample-fluorescence-photoelectric converter display interaction (Fig. 5c). In this case, the concentration of NO2 − was calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation (Equation S1) according to the change in voltage signal converted from the change in fluorescence signal.

The verification of the ability of HSP to detect NO2 − was consistent with that of the fluorescence spectrophotometer, but the concentration of NO2 − added was different. In the range of 10–500 μM, the voltage displayed by the HSP was negatively correlated with NO2 − concentration (Fig. S16). The Stern-Volmer equation was used to describe the relation between them [43]. There was a strong linear correlation between voltage and NO2 − concentration (Fig. 5d). The regression equation was V/V0 = −9.17724 × 10−4X + 0.89669 (R2 = 0.9986). The LOD was 1.95 μM (see SI for details). Although the sensitivity of HSP was lower than that of the fluorometer, the LOD of the HSP was still significantly lower than the maximum limit value of 65 μM for NO2 − in drinking water as ruled by the WHO [25]. Compared with the reported determination methods in Table S1, the HSP could be used to detect NO2 −. Compared with expensive precision instruments, the developed HSP had obvious portability and price advantages and had a good application prospect in field detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − in actual samples using the HSP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table S2, the recovery of water samples was 97 %–100 %. The recovery of meat samples was similar to that of water samples at 96 %–99 %. The results showed that the HSP could be used to accurately detect NO2 − in water and meat samples.

The commercial nitrite analyser was used as a control. After the samples were properly concentrated or diluted as required, the samples were processed in the same way and the concentration of NO2 − was determined according to the standard operating procedures of two devices (HSP and commercial nitrite analyser) to test the difference between the readings of the two devices. The results of the measurement and variance analysis were shown in Table 1 , and there was no significant difference between the two reading devices (P > 0.05). The excessive intake of nitrite would pose a serious threat to the health of human beings. To ensure water quality and human health, it was crucial to track and analyse nitrite in water and food. As shown in Table 1, the HSP could provide relatively reliable detection results in the routine detection of nitrite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the quenching mechanism of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum was the key factor to distinguish dynamic quenching from static quenching. In the quenching process, if the absorption spectrum of N-CDs was unchanged, it was dynamic quenching; otherwise, it was static quenching. However, in the inner filtering effect (IFE) process, the UV–vis spectrum of the quencher would effectively overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of N-CDs. Therefore, the absorption spectrum was an effective method to distinguish IFE, dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanisms [43,44]. To accurately reveal the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis spectra of N-CDs, NaNO2 and N-CDs + NaNO2 were recorded (Fig. 6 a). The comparison between the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3a) and the nitrite UV–vis spectra (Fig. 6a, black line) showed that they did not overlap effectively, which indicated that nitrite could not shield the excitation or emission light of N-CDs. Therefore, the quenching mechanism was not IFE [44]. After adding nitrite, the absorption peaks of N-CDs at 308 and 413 nm were weakened. Moreover, a shoulder peak was observed between 350 and 400 nm (Fig. 6a, blue line), and the first derivative spectrum (Fig. 6b) showed that the maximum absorption of the shoulder peak was at 351 nm. According to the research of Yu et al. [45] and Hao et al. [46], the shoulder peak was the diazonium salt absorption peak. The weakening of the original peak and the appearance of the shoulder peak indicated the formation of diazo compounds [7].

Fluorescence lifetime was an essential property used to discuss the quenching mechanism because it was almost constant in static quenching, but it changed obviously in dynamic quenching [47]. To reveal the accurate quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs + NO2 − systems were determined. The data were in good agreement with the double exponential equation, as shown in Fig. 6c, and the average fluorescence lifetime was given by the sum of the products of ∑biτi [44,47,48]. According to the table in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was 3.97 ns and hardly changed after adding different concentrations of NO2 −, indicating that N-CDs and NO2 − formed a compound in the ground state, which was a static quenching mechanism [43].

Compared with the 1H NMR spectra before (Fig. 6d, black line) and after adding NO2 − (Fig. 6d, red line), the peak of active hydrogen atoms in the amino group with a chemical shift of 6.18 disappeared. Hydrogen atoms with chemical shifts of 6.82 and 6.51 were aromatic, and after NO2 − was added, their chemical shifts moved to high fields of 6.73 and 6.40. The disappearance of amino hydrogen and the obvious chemical shift of adjacent hydrogen in 1H NMR indicated that the amino group on N-CDs had diazotised [35].

The FTIR of products after adding NO2 − was recorded to further reveal its quenching mechanism (Fig. 6e). In the infrared spectrum, the peaks of the N–H stretching vibration at 3356 and 3329 cm−1 and the peaks of the N–H bending vibration at 1598 and 752 cm−1 disappeared, and at the same time, the peak specific to the diazonium group was generated at 2238 cm−1 [1]. All of these proved that the diazotisation reaction of N-CDs occurred and new compounds were generated [49,50].

As shown in Fig. 6f, the zeta potential of N-CDs was 8.01 mV. After adding NO2 −, the zeta potential of the product became 11.90 mV. This might be due to the diazotisation reaction of N-CDs to form positively charged diazonium groups, which increased the electrical potential of N-CDs (in 1.0 mM HCl) [46].

The results of the UV–vis spectrum, FTIR, fluorescence lifetime, NMR and zeta potential showed that –NH2 of N-CDs had undergone diazotisation reaction, the formation of the diazonium group was the inducement of its fluorescence quenching and the quenching of N-CDs by NO2 − was static quenching.

N-CDs with abundant amino groups were prepared using an eco-friendly hydrothermal method, which exhibited good stability, water solubility, low toxicity, high quantum yield (26.46 %) and cell imaging ability with orange-yellow fluorescence. The N-CDs had accurate sensing characteristics for NO2 − that were verified by experiments, which developed a precise determination method for NO2 − based on a fluorescence spectrophotometer in the laboratory. Based on laboratory fluorescence assay, the HSP for NO2 − detection was designed and manufactured using 3D printing technology. The HSP could transform the fluorescent signal emitted by N-CDs into an electrical signal that was easy to detect and display. When NO2 − was determined using the HSP, there was a good linear relation in the range of 10–500 μM with LOD of 1.95 μM, which was lower than the maximum limit value of 65 μM for NO2 − in drinking water as ruled by the WHO. The practical application capability of the sensor was verified in actual water and meat samples, and the reliability of the results was consistent with those of commercial detectors (P > 0.05). The HSP is compact, portable and easily assembled and is suitable for on-site real-time detection, which has great application potential and prospects. In addition, the HSP can be used to accurately detect NO2 − in food and drinking water, thus improving people's diet quality and safety. The essential thing was that this study expanded fluorescence analysis from fluorometer to portable equipment, which provided a new way for developing portable detection equipment for food safety control and environmental analysis.

Liucheng Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yan Zhang: Visualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Liyue Du: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shengrui Xu: Resources, Conceptualization. Jie Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Fengling Cui: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1704170), the Key Programs for Science and Technology Development in Henan Province (212102110164, 222102110130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3
Multimedia component 4 Multimedia component 4

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126527.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Materials, instruments, preparation of solutions, optimization of synthesis of N-CDs, pH stability of N-CDs, the characterization of N-CDs, the kinetics of the reaction between N-CDs and NO2 −, fluorescence spectra and voltage variation of N-CDs quenched by different concentrations of NO2 −, cytotoxicity experiment, cell imaging experiment and recovery experiment of N-CDs, quantum yield of N-CDs, stability experiments and interference experiments were included in the supporting information.
Supporting movies 1-3 (mp4) are the appearance (size), operation video, and internal structure diagram of HSP.
